senses.definition: Having reduced vision in one or both eyes because the brain does not use the eye normally (having amblyopia or lazy eye).
examples: The child was amblyopic in her left eye and needed to wear a patch over the right eye.
An amblyopic patient may not notice the problem until a routine eye examination.
Early treatment can greatly improve sight in an amblyopic eye.

senses.definition: A family of small freshwater cavefish, many of which are blind or nearly blind and live in dark caves and swamps in the eastern United States.
examples: Most species in the Amblyopsidae are adapted to life in complete darkness.
Amblyopsidae includes several cavefish with reduced eyes and lack of pigment.
Researchers surveyed populations of Amblyopsidae in underground streams.

senses.definition: A genus of small, pale, sightless cavefish in the family Amblyopsidae, including the blindfish of Mammoth Cave in the United States.
examples: Amblyopsis is one of the best-known genera of North American cavefish.
The species Amblyopsis spelaea is found in underground streams.
Biologists described new morphological features in Amblyopsis.

senses.definition: An optical instrument used to measure how well the two eyes work together and to train an amblyopic eye to see better.
examples: The orthoptist used an amblyoscope to assess the child’s binocular vision.
Therapy with an amblyoscope can help improve fusion in patients with squint.
Modern clinics often replace the traditional amblyoscope with digital devices.
collocations: angle of deviation on the amblyoscope

senses.definition: Any member of the extinct order Amblypoda, large heavy-bodied hoofed mammals with blunt feet that lived in the early Tertiary period.
examples: The fossil turned out to belong to an amblypod from the Eocene.
Amblypod remains suggest a massive, slow-moving herbivore.
Paleontologists compared the amblypod skeleton with that of modern ungulates.

senses.definition: An extinct order of large, heavy, hoofed mammals, mostly herbivorous, known from early Tertiary rocks.
examples: Amblypoda are often described as large, clumsy ungulates.
The order Amblypoda includes several distinct subgroups.
New finds have reshaped our understanding of Amblypoda evolution.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the characteristics of the extinct mammalian order Amblypoda.
examples: The specimen shows clearly amblypodous features in its limb bones.
These amblypodous traits link the animal to early Tertiary ungulates.
Researchers described the skull as distinctly amblypodous.

senses.definition: A genus of iguanid lizards that includes only the marine iguana Amblyrhynchus cristatus of the Galápagos Islands.
examples: Amblyrhynchus is unique among lizards for its marine lifestyle.
The genus Amblyrhynchus is represented by a single living species.
Studies of Amblyrhynchus have shed light on island evolution.

senses.definition: A technical term in paleontology for a plate or lobe-like part in certain fossil arthropods, especially trilobites.
examples: The amblystegite is clearly visible in the dorsal view of the trilobite.
The researcher noted damage to the amblystegite in several specimens.
Differences in the amblystegite help distinguish these trilobite genera.

senses.definition: A genus of North American salamanders, now usually included in the genus Ambystoma.
examples: Older texts refer to the tiger salamander as Amblystoma tigrinum.
The genus Amblystoma has largely been replaced by Ambystoma in modern taxonomy.
Amblystoma species are important models in developmental biology.

senses.definition: In some Christian churches, a raised stand or platform from which the Bible is read or a sermon is given.
examples: The lector approached the ambo to read the first lesson.
Fresh flowers decorated the ambo for the Easter service.
The priest preached from the ambo during the mass.

senses.definition: Similar to or having the properties of an amboceptor in immunology.
examples: The amboceptoid activity of the serum was measured experimentally.
Researchers described an amboceptoid substance in the patient’s blood.
These amboceptoid factors may enhance complement fixation.

senses.definition: An antibody that can attach both to an antigen and to complement, helping to destroy cells such as bacteria or red blood cells.
examples: The hemolytic reaction depends on the presence of an amboceptor and complement.
Each amboceptor has a site for the cell and a site for the complement component.
Early immunologists used the term amboceptor for what we now call specific antibodies.

senses.definition: Plural of amboceptor.
examples: Amboceptors bind to red cells and then attract complement.
The serum contained high levels of amboceptors after immunization.
Amboceptors play a central role in the classical complement pathway.

senses.definition: A rare technical term in zoology for a simple body cavity or an early stage of coelom development in some animals.
examples: The embryo passes through an ambocoelia stage during development.
Ambocoelia formation precedes the division of the coelomic cavities.
Researchers described the ambocoelia in the larval anatomy.

senses.definition: An old legal term for a lawyer who takes fees from both sides in a case, in a dishonest way.
examples: He was accused of acting as an ambodexter in the dispute.
The statute punished any ambodexter who betrayed his client’s trust.
In medieval records, an ambodexter was despised as corrupt.

senses.definition: An older English name for Ambon, an island in eastern Indonesia.
examples: The spice trade once made Amboina an important colonial outpost.
Historical maps often label the island as Amboina.
The Amboina massacre was a key event in Dutch–English rivalry.

senses.definition: Plural of Amboina, referring to the island in historical or geographical contexts.
examples: The chart listed Ternate and the Amboinas among key spice islands.
Writers of the period spoke of the Bandas and the Amboinas together.
Claims over the Amboinas led to conflict between European powers.

senses.definition: Relating to Ambon (Amboina) Island or its people.
examples: They collected traditional Amboinese songs.
Amboinese spices were highly valued in Europe.
The study focused on Amboinese cultural practices.

senses.definition: A person from Ambon (Amboina) Island.
examples: An Amboinese served as interpreter for the Dutch.
Many Amboinese migrated to the Netherlands in the twentieth century.
The museum includes portraits of prominent Amboinese.

senses.definition: Relating to or caused by an embolus, especially a blood clot blocking a vessel.
examples: The patient suffered ambolic complications after surgery.
Ambolic events in the brain can lead to stroke.
Doctors monitored for possible ambolic phenomena.

senses.definition: Relating to both the incus (anvil) and malleus (hammer) bones in the middle ear.
examples: The surgeon examined the ambomalleal joint carefully.
Ambomalleal fixation can impair hearing.
The study described several ambomalleal abnormalities.

senses.definition: A city and island in eastern Indonesia, part of the Maluku Islands.
examples: Ambon is known for its role in the historical spice trade.
They flew from Jakarta to Ambon for fieldwork.
Ambon has a tropical climate with high rainfall.

senses.definition: Plural of ambo, referring to raised reading stands in churches.
examples: The cathedral has two stone ambones facing the nave.
Ancient mosaics decorate the ambones in the basilica.
The restoration included both pulpits and ambones.

senses.definition: A trade name for a type of synthetic resin or plastic material.
examples: The casing was molded from Ambonite for durability.
Ambonite sheets were used as an insulating layer.
The company patented Ambonite as a fire-resistant plastic.

senses.definition: A village and Grand Cru vineyard area in the Champagne region of France.
examples: Ambonnay is famous for its Pinot Noir grapes.
They visited small producers in Ambonnay during their wine tour.
The label notes that the wine comes from Ambonnay vineyards.

senses.definition: Plural of ambo, stands used for reading in some churches.
examples: The historic church preserves its original ambos.
Carved wooden ambos flank the altar.
The designer drew inspiration from Romanesque ambos.

senses.definition: Having both male and female sexual characteristics; hermaphroditic.
examples: The species is ambosexous and can self-fertilize.
Ambosexous organisms may have reproductive advantages in isolated habitats.
The worm’s ambosexous anatomy was carefully described.

senses.definition: Having or involving both sexes; hermaphroditic.
examples: The plant has ambosexual flowers.
Ambosexual reproduction is common in some invertebrates.
The term ambosexual has been used in older biological texts.

senses.definition: Another spelling of Amboina, an older English name for Ambon Island in Indonesia.
examples: Amboyna appears frequently in seventeenth-century travel accounts.
The Amboyna massacre strained relations between the English and Dutch.
The treaty settled competing claims over Amboyna.

senses.definition: Plural of Amboyna, used in older geographical and historical writings.
examples: The map showed the Bandas and the Amboynas.
Control of the Amboynas was crucial for the spice monopolies.
Merchants sailed regularly to the Amboynas.

senses.definition: Relating to the ancient gulf or region of Ambracia in western Greece.
examples: The fleet anchored in Ambracan waters.
Ambracan towns prospered under Roman rule.
The poem celebrates an Ambracan victory at sea.

senses.definition: A fragrant resinous substance obtained from ambergris and used in making perfume.
examples: The chemist isolated ambrain from the ambergris sample.
Ambrain contributes to the long-lasting base notes of the perfume.
Synthetic ambrain has largely replaced the natural product.

senses.definition: A chemical salt or ester derived from ambric or amber-related acids (used in older chemical terminology).
examples: The compound was identified as a sodium ambreate.
Ambreates were once used in certain fragrance formulations.
The sample contained traces of an organic ambreate.

senses.definition: Relating to ambergris or its chemical derivatives.
examples: Ambreic acids were isolated from the waxy mass.
The perfumer experimented with ambreic compounds.
Ambreic substances give the fragrance a warm, rich tone.

senses.definition: A triterpene alcohol that is a main component of ambergris and is used in making perfumes.
examples: Ambrein is responsible for many of the fixative properties of ambergris.
The lab synthesized ambrein from plant-based precursors.
Ambrein derivatives are common in modern perfumery.

senses.definition: A tropical plant whose seeds have a musky smell and are used in flavoring and perfumery; also the seeds themselves.
examples: The perfumer added ambrette to the floral accord.
Ambrette seeds lend a soft musk note to the scent.
Traditional medicine in some regions uses ambrette seeds.

senses.definition: A synthetic musk fragrance compound, originally derived from ambrette, used in many perfumes.
examples: Ambrettolide gives the perfume a sweet, musky base.
Modern formulations often rely on ambrettolide rather than natural musk.
The safety of ambrettolide has been evaluated by fragrance committees.

senses.definition: An old or rare place or personal name found in historical or literary sources.
examples: The charter records a grant of land near Ambrica.
Little is known about the exact location of Ambrica.
The heroine of the romance is called Ambrica.

senses.definition: Plural of ambry, a small cupboard or recess in a church wall, often used to store sacred items.
examples: The stone ambries in the chancel still contain ancient vessels.
Medieval churches often have one or more wall ambries.
The guide pointed out the painted doors of the ambries.

senses.definition: A trade name for a type of synthetic amber-like resin or plastic.
examples: The beads were made of Ambrite rather than natural amber.
Ambrite became popular as an affordable substitute for amber.
Collectors can usually distinguish Ambrite from true amber.

senses.definition: A pressed or molded plastic made from small pieces of amber or from synthetic resin, used as an amber imitation.
examples: The necklace is made of ambroid, not real amber.
Early pipe stems were sometimes carved from ambroid.
Ambroid became fashionable in the early twentieth century.

senses.definition: Plural of ambroid.
examples: The shop sold ambroids alongside genuine amber pieces.
Old catalogs list several types of ambroids for industrial use.
Collectors examine ambroids carefully to avoid confusion with real amber.

senses.definition: The scientific study of amber.
examples: Ambrology sheds light on ancient ecosystems preserved in resin.
She published an introduction to ambrology for geology students.
Modern ambrology combines geology, chemistry, and paleontology.

senses.definition: A male given name.
examples: Ambrose arrived early for the meeting.
Her grandfather Ambrose was a quiet man.
The novel’s main character is called Ambrose.

senses.definition: In Greek and Roman mythology, the food of the gods, thought to give them immortality.
examples: The poet wrote of ambrosia served on golden plates.
Ambrosia and nectar were reserved for the gods.
Heroes sometimes tasted ambrosia in mythic tales.

senses.definition: Food that tastes extremely good.
examples: After the long hike, the simple soup tasted like ambrosia.
To a hungry traveler, even plain bread can be ambrosia.
The dessert was pure ambrosia for chocolate lovers.

senses.definition: Having the delicious taste or smell of ambrosia; exceptionally pleasing to the senses.
examples: The chef prepared an ambrosiac meal.
An ambrosiac aroma filled the kitchen.
They enjoyed an ambrosiac feast by the sea.

senses.definition: A former plant family name that included ragweeds and related genera, now usually treated as part of the Asteraceae.
examples: Older classifications list ragweeds under Ambrosiaceae.
Ambrosiaceae is now merged into the larger Asteraceae family.
The pollen of Ambrosiaceae species is highly allergenic.

senses.definition: Relating to plants of the genus Ambrosia or the former family Ambrosiaceae.
examples: Ambrosiaceous weeds are common along the roadside.
The allergen is present in several ambrosiaceous species.
Ambrosiaceous flora contributes to seasonal hay fever.

senses.definition: Extremely pleasant to taste or smell, as if it were food of the gods.
examples: The cake had an ambrosial aroma.
We enjoyed an ambrosial dinner on the terrace.
The air was filled with ambrosial scents from the garden.

senses.definition: In a way that is extremely pleasant to taste or smell, like ambrosia.
examples: The sauce smelled ambrosially as it simmered.
The fruit was ambrosially sweet.
The kitchen was ambrosially perfumed with spices.
